3. Karlton and Jared - Tax Alchemy.mp4 So for those of you guys that aren't familiar with me, my name is Carlton Dennis. I'm a licensed enrolled agent. I'm not a CPA, but I'm a tax strategist. And I got started back in 2000. In 2017, when I joined my mother's firm, I learned firsthand how she was helping everyday entrepreneurs save a shit ton of money in taxes. All she did was tax strategy. She didn't choose to just file tax returns. She was actually helping people leverage the tax code. And we decided to create Tax Alchemy, a more boutique version of her firm. In 2020, after I learned more about real estate strategies and the power of investing in real estate. So I'm going to talk to you guys a little bit more about how we scale to. million dollar months and turn it over to Jared to go into the granular details, but just kind of high level right now. We're at about 14 million gross so far in 2000. 2025 we'll probably finish the year somewhere around like 17 to 18 million. We're about to do a challenge coming up in the next Like 10 15 days. So we're selling tickets for that. We're supposed to get somewhere around 1 ,200 to 1 ,300 tickets. And if all things go well, we'll probably do anywhere between two and a half, hopefully. upwards to 3 million cash collected on that challenge. Earlier this year, I hit a million subscribers on YouTube. All of what I do is organic. If you guys have seen... some of my content online. I started my YouTube channel in 2021, right when COVID was kicking off. Of the first videos on YouTube around how to pay yourself as an LLC, that video just went straight viral, and then I started doing research on Google Trends of what are the things that that people are researching, as it pertains to taxes. And then I just started creating YouTube videos for absolutely everything. I viewed YouTube as digital real estate. If there's gonna be a video on taxes, I wanted to have a video on it, whether it was playing yourself through an Escort, placing a child on payroll, riding off a G-Wagon, or riding off a Tesla. I wanted to have a video for it. Our Instagram channel starts... to grow when we uh had school of hard knocks run into me when I was in Beverly Hills, sitting inside of my Ferrari, and that video went to a hundred. Shortly after that, I had rappers and ballplayers and all types of entertainers reaching out to us, and our business grew even more. 2022 was when I did my first challenge business. I had never done a five-day challenge before, but I got connected with an entrepreneur named Nehemiah Davis, who lives out in Atlanta. And he taught me the entire challenge business. And so I was able to bum his sales team off of him, get all the SOPs, learn everything. And after we cracked a million on my very first challenge, I had the playbook and I started running my own challenges. But the issue was, is that. I never hit million-dollar months without it just being me. It wasn't until around end of 2022, 2023 that I met Jared and we started growing our sales team. And now our sales team is upwards to about eight or nine employees. And now we're finally hitting million-dollar months. And I'll let him go into all of those details. As an entrepreneur, what I'm focused on for 2026 is: we have a book launch. We're going to leverage that book launch into another challenge that's going to come up in March. I'm building a media company because. I realized is that I'm the main show. I looked at the Dan Martell model of how Dan grew his business so fast. How many of you guys know who Dan Martell is? The book Buy Back Your Time, Dan strategically came out with that book and simultaneously started his media company right at the exact same time. And he went from doing talking head videos. Which is a lot of what I do, to having videographers following him around every single day in real time. You're being able to see a completely different lifestyle of Dan. Trust recession right now. And although I have a lot of credibility putting out YouTube videos and talking about tax for the last couple of years, trust is being diminished right now. The internet we have all types of people that are scamming people you name it and so I just want to make sure I can focus on doubling down on what I know that has worked well that has got us here which is media. So we're building a media company right now. And then we're testing new pricing models. We've only had one real core offer for like forever, which is, it worked in our favor because everyone knows what our offer is. It's been $32,000 for forever. But now we're incorporating a higher ticket offer that incorporates tax preparation, bookkeeping and accounting. It just makes it a one-stop shop for absolutely everything versus having things segregated. But that's pretty much everything I wanted to cover today. Just kind of kept a high level. I'll do some Q &A at the end and I'll turn it over to Jerry to kind of go into more of the granular numbers. What's going on, guys? To be fair, I am not a tax person, so please do not ask me tax questions. I am a sales, marketing, and systems guy, so I'm hoping I can just share some of that information with you guys of what has helped us get to where we are today. Trust recession. Let's talk about this. I think Jeremy touched on this a little bit earlier today, but 20 years ago was very institutional. Today, I feel we are in a bit of a trust recession. And I think. People are just looking to trust people, not necessarily an institution anymore. OK, that looks like consistency, authenticity, and proof. And in this environment, just what Carlton said. Buyers are looking for ways to trust you guys online, right? I think Jeremy's exercise with ChatGPT was a great one. And so, really, what we're trying to do is walk them to their yes. Engineer their consent. If you do not know what engineering consent is, the most famous real-world example of this was in the 1920s around women smoking cigarettes. Look that up on ChatGPT. I think you'll find that very, very interesting. We try to incorporate that into our emails, our marketing, and everything we do when walking people to a yes. Here's a little bit of a high-level overview of where we were last year versus where we are this year. So, 2024, we focused on high volume but low precision. Really, what that means to us is we had a 12K product. It was called TA+. We sold about 100 deals into that. And then we had PCG, which is our 32K product. That is our primary flagship product that we sell. And the reality was that in 2024, we had $3. 4 million in sales. Again, it was a good, really good growth year for us. 2023, we did $5 million. 2024, we did $12 million. So again, still a really, really awesome year for us, but a lot of that revenue is still driven by challenges. So the year of 2025, the biggest thing that we're trying to focus on is how do we create a more consistent and predictable sales process and sales team. So we're not so reliant on a heavy influx of 120 clients coming in through a challenge, which I'm sure. As you guys can imagine, selling a 32K high-ticket product like that, it does put a little stress on your back-end systems and your people. So again, going into this year, we focused on... high volume and high precision. We sold a version of a 12k product we varied this year, we gave people a tax plan, but what we noticed was that ultimately we sold very few of these, and it truth be told led to 90% of our headaches on the back end. Whereas we focused on a lot more high volume, high precision with our core product, PCG. And that is actually 160 deals as of now. Sales team closed another two. Those here today working on a Saturday love to see that, but again, just to be clear, we do not do any funding at all. This is a pure 32k PIF product. They got to have 100k liquid capital to join our program at all, because again, you got to spend money to reduce some taxes, right? So that's a little bit of a snapshot of kind of like what we've been doing this year. Some additional information on that. You know, again, this year we're at 7 million cash collected. Now, keep in mind, that is not challenge included. This is only our sales team. So, again, there's a huge, huge focus for us. Revenue last year was a 40% contributor versus a 10% contributor this year. We're probably going to completely discontinue that product altogether next year. 32K will be our base product and starting point going into next year. And as again, you can see PCG revenue, 160 plus sales. We have doubled that this year, which has led to arguably most of our revenue growth this year as well. A couple of things here. Jeremy spoke about certainty earlier. When I joined the community in July, The only thing I was certain about is our ability to take action effectively and immediately. And the first thing I had personal immediate control over and the ability to take action on was the hammer them email system. Everything else we did, such as the retargeting videos, the thank you page video, that did rely very heavily on Carlton and his content schedule. And so, as you guys know, we're very heavy on Instagram, YouTube, you name it. He's always producing content. When he's not doing long form, he's doing... short form. He's not doing short form; he's doing again more long form. So I knew I had immediate control of that and so, in July, we had a little bit of a dip in our sales. I think we're at about 720,000. And we immediately implemented those emails. And then that month of August, that was the one thing we implemented that was statistically improved. And in August, we had our first million-dollar month. We went to about just shy of 1. 2 million with that first implementation. Following that, we then hit them with the retargeting videos and the breakout thank you page videos. So the breakout thank you page videos, I think, has been tremendously helpful for us in a couple different ways. Number one. People are canceling their calls with us because they understand better what our qualifications are. We're not wasting time on these calls of people who are disqualified. They don't have 100K liquid. Opening up more space for our sales team to actually have qualified people. Now, a subsequent result of that is we have a 60% show up rate on our setter and closer side. So that is something we absolutely have to. Improve on our end, but even with that, we are clearing up that space automatically. And people are just hey— we're not; they're telling us they're not qualified. Maybe next year's a better time. It allows us to put them on a list and follow up with them. Then, these are the three things that we have immediately implemented within these two months. And again, as of today, we're at about 650, 50,000 cash collected. Trying to get that 1. 5 million mark. Outside of our challenge, as well, to really end off the year. And I forgot who spoke just before us. Urgency. And I'll just say urgency. We cut off our sales October 31st this year. After this month, we are no longer onboarding anybody else. And then we go back to our back-end sales. Our back-end sales is... where we start to resell all of our back-end clients. They came on in 2024. We're going to resell them into either a PIF or a monthly model that allows them to renew with us and continue that provided value and continue to come through that pathway. VSL, we are very simple in this. Honestly, we spend about $5,000 a day right now on a VSL, and that has had, on average, a 4x return on Aspen for us. Generated about $1. 4 million in revenue. And I'm sure you guys can do the math. That means $5. 5 million of that other revenue has come from our organic content. So I think you guys can start to understand. Why Carlton's media and what he produces is so effective on our part. And what this also tells me is that we have barely scratched the surface on our advertising efforts and how much we can squeeze out of these ad platforms when it comes to what we're spending on the BSL. So as you can see, most of our budget is allocated to Meta. Truth be told, we haven't really figured out the paid YouTube strategy yet. We do very good organically, but we haven't quite figured out the paid side of YouTube yet. And again, all this is— this is a gated piece of content. So they get on there, they have to watch 10 minutes before we even let them release and see the rest of the page or move on to the actual application. From there, they fill out an application. We either qualify, disqualify. If they're qualified, they go directly to a booking. And we take it on from there. Same thing with our organic content, except we don't make them go through a VSL. Because they're coming through our organic content, they typically are already consuming a majority of our content. We don't want to have to them regurgitate the content that they're already been seeing and watching. This is where that comes in now. One thing I want to notice here: um, we did. After joining Jeremy's community, June was our biggest month up until then. We did $820,000 cash collected in June, and we're playing with our sales system a little bit more there. Of a hybrid model where we are very restrictive of who could get on a closed call and who could not get on a closed call. And so we're making them either watch very gated content, which is an hour and 40-minute webinar, a pre-call video. Some sort, but we're making sure that they had some level of education before they got to that call. But then, of course, you guys know there's a type 2 individual out there who's not going to go do this stuff. You probably couldn't even them to go do this stuff until they understand why they need to go do it. And so we would help push these people through to the closer counters. Because fundamentally, closers have a much better principle standard of how they can prescribe information to people and help them come to those conclusions on their own. So what we did and what we looked at was, in June, our VSL had an 87-day average close from the time they came in the door to the time they actually paid us money. Looking at the last 30 days, pretty much September, that went down to 30 days for us. So we essentially, by implementing these strategies, we reduced our... speed to lead and really just led to close by about 60 days. Organic stayed very similar, but where we saw the biggest drop was from the initial time that they booked a call with us. We saw a 20-day drop from the time they actually closed with us. So again, it did speed up the sales cycle a little bit, but we have a lot of improvement to do with our actual speed to lead. I've learned a lot of stuff from Ryan actually about how setters should be reaching out to people. So I absolutely love that. And I think that's stuff we're going to be focusing on here going into 2026. Sales teams. I think this is a really, really important one. I think it's often overlooked more often than not. And I think there's really a few key things that drive performance, number one values, integrity, urgency, and accountability, especially accountability. If you don't have an accountable sales team, they're going to just kind of transparently bitch and complain about the leads that are showing up on their calendars. We use Dan Martell's 1-3-1 method very, very heavily. One problem, three solutions. What is the one thing we are going to execute on? That is a fundamental principle that we apply to all of our sales and structure. And then as we speak today, we have three closes and four setters and our closes close at about a 32% close rate. And we have about a 5,500 cash per booking as we speak. 60% show rate. So that's kind of how we're operating at the moment. We operate lean and mean. Closers, just as a heads up there, we have a bit of a different pay structure. It's very similar to kind of what Ryan was saying, but we don't have bonus structures or anything like that. We have a tiered cash collected structure. So the first deal is always 8%. After that, up to 84K cash collected is 10%. After that, 84K is 12% to 120. And then from 120 and above is 15%. Allowed some of our closes to make $60,000 a month in some of our highest months. So I think that's absolutely incredible. It's allowed us to retain these people. Another nugget I'm going to give you guys here is we don't use a recruiting agency. If any of you guys have heard of Unicorn Closer Mastermind, Josh Lyons hosts that mastermind. Where we pull people from. Skyler, he's actually our lead sales rep. He joined that community, $3,000 a month. So all of our closers, it's actually almost mandatory now that they do that. It's an investment in themselves. They pay that $3,000 a month. And based off their results, all three of our clothes are in there. We actually have a couple setters in there. We pulled people from there. We pulled a setter from there beginning of September. He did 12. $12,000 is first month with us when normally there's at least a 60-day ramp-up period with us. So we found that to be incredibly, incredibly valuable. They are not a placement agency. They're just a training hub. For incredibly good closers. And really what we do is we do scriptless selling. We do not have a script. And I will say Carlton was pretty shocked to hear that the first time. And I don't think it's— stoked about that, but it has led to more conversation-based selling rather than just sounding like a robot. And again, just going through the motions there. So that's been really, really effective for us. And then, yeah, pretty much same thing, right? Speed to lead, recruiting from that network. Because our closes have had such great success, even with our setters, we actually had a setter last month make 30K. Because he had 500 cash collected with us, which is awesome. We have literally a wait list of people in that community waiting to want to come close for us, which again, I think is the best thing in the world for us as a company as we want to scale and grow. But we do operate a very lean sales team as we don't want to overexpand. How many of those things? Brand building drives sales. And I can't really stress this enough. You know, Carlton's brand has generated a lot of trust through the consistency of him showing up day in and day out for his community and the people he's talking to. And so this has been tremendous in our sales process. Again, as you guys can see, we spent $5,000 a day on the VSL but most of our sales still do come from organic sources. So all we're doing is just trying to extend that branding and that messaging through our marketing, pull more people into our community, and let them kind of go from there. It's funny to look back at this now, knowing what we know today, is that a webinar to sell a $97 ticket or a $297 ticket is an extremely friction process. It still works, don't get me wrong, but we found that this year, a long-form sales page has been way less friction and way less effort from Carlton's end. When we were doing 2024 selling, Carlton was selling for 60 days straight, two live webinars a week. That was every Wednesday and every Saturday to get to those 1. 2K tickets. Sales and yeah, that's a crack— three million though— on that challenge that's right, August, August of 2024, we did 3. 5 million revenue. Um, so that was our biggest challenge yet. We did about $2. 5 million revenue. All of those challenges have been previously five-day challenges. And so I'm sure, as you guys can imagine, most people do a three-day challenge. We lost a lot of people after that third day. and we don't even pitch people until day four. So this time here in October, like Carlton said, in a week and a half, we are running our first three-day model of a challenge. It's gonna be a more... a production studio like this where there's a screen stage, some faces, kind of like the Al Carmosini style, and we are going to be selling a 50k offer. And just so you guys are aware, we always pitch the 50k off with a 5k deposit, and we always down sell into there based off liquidity, if they do not have the capacity to go beyond that. So That's about all I have for you guys. Happy to answer any questions that you guys may have around this. I will say one thing. Everything about what we're doing with this new challenge is new, right? Doing challenges for the last two or three years the exact same way. Like literally the exact same way. I'm saying the exact same thing on every single challenge. Only done five day challenges, I'm the only person on my entire challenge I emcee my entire challenge, but because I've been a part of the group, I've learned some new things. I've been networking with some new people. Now we're going to a three-day challenge. Instead of me sitting at my desk, I'm going to be sitting on a stage like this with a media board. Instead of me being the only speaker, I have Ryan Pineda and Pace Morby and all these different people coming in to speak. I'm going to have an MC. and I'm also still spending about three to half a million dollars— 300 to about half a million dollars— on this challenge. I'm just saying, like, I'm willing to try new all the time. Like, I will always try. I knew shit and it's because of the people who I've surrounded myself with and how fast people are moving that I just want to get on the other side of, like, whatever the shit is. You know what I'm saying? So, if the three-day works, great. If it doesn't work, I'm right back to my five-day. If me being on stage works, great. If it doesn't, I'm right back to my computer desk in front of my iPad. I just need to try new shit as quickly as possible so I can figure out what is successful. Questions? What is the way? Why do you think you can get people and you want to get them on the floor and go right here? Could you say it a little bit louder? I'm sorry, I couldn't hear you. Why do you need to play guitar on occasion? What's your name? What? Why do I wait until day four? Is that what you're saying? Honestly, the model that was taught to me is to pitch on day four. So with our five-day challenge model, day one is: complete mindset training. So you're just reframing somebody's mind. Then day two, I'm going into advanced value. My biggest advanced value day is typically on day two. Day three is when I'm dropping an application link. I'm saying, 'Hey, some of you guys have already started reaching out to me. You guys are reaching out to my team. Like, dude, how do I work with you guys on this stuff? Listen. And I'm not saying that we're going to open anything up, but I will put an application. If you guys are curious enough to want to know what I'm going to pitch, at least drop an application. I'll see if I'm going to open it up. And then, when we come on to day four, I have testimonials on day four.' So now they're getting to hear from like, I'll probably have anywhere from six to seven people, six to seven of my clients. And I'll bring them on to what I call an inspiration panel. So they'll all come up at once, and I'll go one by one, and I'll ask them, 'Hey, tell me a little bit about why you decided to join Tax Alchemy. You were here on this challenge, literally, a year ago. What were the problems that you were trying to solve and how is Taxol going to be able to help you solve them? And I go one by one, and I choose those testimonials specifically.' To objections or limiting beliefs that I know are going to be in the audience, whether it's over time, whether it's over fear of the IRS, I already have a CPA. What if all this doesn't work out? Like I'm choosing. Different types of testimonials and strategically planting them into Day Four. And then I pitch. Day Four is a pitch. Day Five, I come back, I teach something, and then I'm pitching all Day Five. If I don't close up, however many I wanted to close up on Day Five, then I'll do a surprise bonus training. Then I'll come back on the Saturday and do a bonus training. And then, if I am really feeling like there's still juice left, then I'll do a AMA on a Monday. So I'll come back on the Monday and do an AMA. Oh my gosh, I wasn't able to get to everybody's questions. Carlton's going to do an Ask Me Anything (AMA). And then I bring everybody that my sales team couldn't close over the weekend to an AMA, and then I close them. And wrap everything up. So that's how we've done it. Our challenge business, I would say, does like a 4X ROAS on ad spend. So, yeah. So you get your 32K offer on day four, and you get the call to action five days prior to a sales call, and you're like, 'Let's see how you call that.' Now, I'm not saying that we got cash collected, all 170 people, but that was a big... deposit day for us. Then those people were able to book calls with my sales team that night and then with my sales team the following day, we only extended out 24 hours for them to book. So I'm glad you asked that because, to be honest with you, when I first ran the challenge business, it was more like, it was more like a mastermind because the person who was teaching it to me told me, 'You're going to run. Mastermind for a year and people are going to sign up, call it something.' So I called it the tax-free wealth mastermind. Well, people were in this idea that I'm going to go learn something. And then, after I'm done learning, I don't work with Carlton anymore. So I completely went away from the whole mastermind concept and I started calling it PCG, private client group. Now people realize, okay, I'm a client of Carlton's, right? That was just like the first transition I had. I'm like, okay, now they're clients. How do I get them to renew? It wasn't until last year, in like August, that I was like, 'When I'm pitching, I'm going to make sure that they understand, in the engagement agreement, there's a place for them to automatically renew.' So when you sign the contract for $32,000, you'll sign for the year. But after the year is over, then you go to a month. monthly fee, which is $1,500 a month, and you can initial right there on the contract. So I'm technically already locking you in for the renewal when you onboard now. Renewal rate right now is at 68%. We're hoping to get that up to 87% by the end of 2026. Yes. One, two, three, four. Og så ses vi ellers, hvis det gælder for os. How do we train our closers to close on such a sophisticated problem? Yeah, I think that's a great question. So sales principles first, I want to be clear. None of our salespeople are tax professionals at all, right? So that's the greatest way to get in the weeds with some of these people. Really, we want to identify it's like, and give you a perspective here. We get people have paid two hundred fifty-five hundred thousand dollars in taxes last ten years and they have yet to conceptualize that they have just given anywhere from two to $5 million to the IRS over that amount of time until they really get on a call with us. And so, you know, we really want to break down. It's like, why? Do you want help with this now? And so that's where I say it's like. I personally don't do a lot of the training myself. That's where we pull from. The Unicorn Closer Mastermind, specifically. Because that's essentially an incubator in my mind. Number one: pulling closes from there. They're already paying for their own growth and investment. Which I think is very very pivotal. If you're not, I think you can be very stagnant in a sales process. And as a sales rep, but fundamentally, it's a lot of discovery. Um and so that's where I say it's like. We've found great success from pulling from that community. Personally, and people are like, kind of lining up the door for us. But yeah, I mean, we're not like heavy on like going into strategies, ETC. Really want to identify. It's like, what are you looking to achieve with this? Why do you want to help now? Like, you've been paying 500. Cave for the last five years like what is it today that has caused you to want to change that situation? Right, so a lot of discoveries, a lot of people, kind of come into that realization on their own. A little bit and that's what a lot of our value-based emails are as well as helping people. You know, I had a conversation with Jeremy pretty much the second I got into the community, and he said, 'You want to generate content that inspires people to take action.' And so he kind of, he held up an example. He's like, if you look at a Rubik's cube, whatever he held up, he's like, 'You know, people see it from this perspective, but you might explain it from the backside of what you see, but they don't see that. So they can't conceptualize what you're trying to describe to them.' And so you have to find. Find a way to describe things from their perspective and how they may see it and shift that thinking into a manner of 'now is the time to take action on this and really change their situation.' And truly understanding the outcomes too, right? Like my sales reps are never going to become tax strategists. And to be completely honest with you, the more they dive into the tax code, the further they get from actually making sales. They start complicating the shit. And I don't need them to become tax professionals trying to solve somebody's problem on a phone call. I need them to identify the problem on a phone call, right? And so they... are educated on the outcomes of our strategies. They have to be able to get on a phone call and talk intelligently about an LLC or an S corporation, of course, but they really understand the outcomes. They don't understand the full details of what goes into tax and accounting. Question. Terima kasih kerana menonton! The advantage of taking in a deposit. Is that for the challenge? Yeah. Just to kind of lock in that commitment, right? Because otherwise you get a lot of people that just, you know, book unintentionally. You know, we thought about doing that in a regular sales process. But again, we don't want to give people a... longer picture of thinking that they're going to get tax advice on it. Again, that's not the principle of the call. The principle of the deposit is really just get the engagement and the commitment. That's really important to us. And again, it's a completely refundable deposit. If we get on the call with you, honestly, if people don't communicate with us from the first two days, we automatically just refund it. We just, here's your money back. See you later. Like you'll come back. And if we put the full amount and like what happens if their credit card declines, right? Like $3,000, is very low that your credit card is going to decline. I just want you to hit that $3,000 and I get on the phone with my salesperson. A salesperson can determine how to get that payment done. Now they're on the call with you when your credit card declines, and they're giving you a different solution. Oh, well, let's wire. Oh, well, let's try a different business route, right? Yeah. He said that during these sales, I was like, we go quick street, challenge the yacht sales, call the eubosia. Are you dropping? Vice the mediator's new card. No, absolutely not. I'm letting them know the price is going to go up right after the call with me. So when they come all the way back around, so I do my five-day challenge. I do a bonus day. I don't close as many as I wanted to on the five-day challenge. I normally go about 10 to 12 deposits on that bonus day. But then some people will lose faith over the weekend. They'll start refunding, whatever. So I might lose 10 to 12 people on that Sunday, right? They went to church, found God, and then all of a sudden they don't want to do tax strategy anymore. I come back on Monday, and do an AMA call. So anybody that was doubtful or my sales team is saying, so-and-so is not trying to move forward anymore, or they're still on the fence, they put their deposit down, but we can't get a hold of them. We route all of those people to the AMA call. And when they're on the AMA call with me, I let them know, 'Hey.' This deal literally was only supposed to last until the end of the weekend. I'm going to extend it here today. Don't believe me? Here's somebody who paid $50,000 literally two weeks ago. I'm literally only doing this offer for you guys while I'm on this challenge.
